text.title: Three A/B Test Analysis Examples
text.summary: Worked examples of significant result, insufficient data (needs more), and underpowered test.

example.code:
Test: New checkout button color (gray → green)
Data: A (gray): n1=15,000, x1=450 → p1=3.00% B (green): n2=15,000, x2=525 → p2=3.50%
Calculation: p_pooled = 975/30000 = 0.0325 SE = sqrt(0.0325 * 0.9675 * (1/15000 + 1/15000)) = 0.00129 z = (0.035 - 0.030) / 0.00129 = 3.88 p-value = 0.0001
Result: p < 0.05 → Statistically significant 95% CI: [+0.25%, +0.75%] — zero not in interval Decision: SHIP green button

section.title: Example 2: Not Significant — Need More Data

example.code:
Test: Headline copy change
Data: A: n1=2,000, x1=100 → p1=5.0% B: n2=2,000, x2=110 → p2=5.5%
Calculation: p_pooled = 0.0525 SE = 0.00158 z = 0.317 p-value = 0.75
Result: p > 0.05 → NOT significant 95% CI: [-2.6%, +3.6%] — interval includes zero Decision: Need ~10,000 per variant to detect this 0.5% absolute difference
example.note: Do not report this as "no effect". It means "insufficient evidence". The true lift could be anywhere in the CI range.

example.code:
Setup: Baseline: 10%, MDE: 5% relative (0.5% absolute) Actual sample: 500 per variant
Required sample calculation: n = 16 * 0.10 * 0.90 / 0.005^2 = 57,600 per variant
Actual power: ~6% (only 6% chance of detecting the real effect)
Conclusion: This test was essentially useless. Even if B is genuinely better, there was a 94% chance of missing it with this sample.
example.note: Running this test wasted 1,000 user-exposures and produced an untrustworthy null result. Always compute required sample size before launch.
